blocks.text: Throwing a computer in a skip is not just lazy — it's a liability. Those hard drives almost certainly still contain recoverable data. And the lead, mercury, and cadmium in that hardware will be in someone's groundwater within a decade. When we talk about ethical technology, we mean the entire lifecycle: from design to disposal. End-of-life is not someone else's problem.
Why E-Waste Is Harmful

Electronic devices contain materials that are both valuable and hazardous. Lead in circuit boards, mercury in screens, and cadmium in batteries can all cause severe environmental and human health damage when they leach out of landfill into soil and groundwater. Informal e-waste recycling in developing countries — where Australian e-waste is sometimes illegally shipped — exposes workers to toxic fumes from burning cables and circuit boards.

rows: Neurological damage, especially in children; soil and water contamination
Severe neurological damage; bioaccumulates in food chains
Kidney damage, bone disease; very persistent in soil
Toxic fumes when incinerated; endocrine disruption
Mining causes environmental destruction; finite global supply

blocks.text: Responsible Disposal Options

rows: Free recycling at authorised collection points for computers and TVs
Return old devices to the manufacturer for responsible recycling
Phones, printers, batteries — check brand programs
Donate working equipment to schools, charities, or community programs
Commercial recycling companies certified to safely process hazardous materials
Households with items not covered by NTCRS

blocks.text: Data Security Before Disposal

Disposing of hardware without first sanitising storage media is a serious data breach risk. Simply deleting files or emptying the recycle bin does not remove data — it only removes the file index. The underlying data remains accessible with freely available recovery software. Effective data sanitisation must occur before any device leaves your control.

items: Factory reset alone is insufficient for secure disposal — it does not wipe flash storage fully
Use NIST 800-88 compliant wiping software (e.g., DBAN) to overwrite all storage sectors
For highly sensitive data, physical destruction of the drive (shredding or degaussing) is the most secure option
Document the disposal process — who destroyed what, when, and by what method — for audit trail purposes
For BYOD (Bring Your Own Device) policies, ensure corporate data partitions are wiped when an employee leaves

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · E-WASTE DISPOSAL PLAN
blocks.text: The government office has 200 computers to dispose of. Draft a step-by-step disposal plan that covers: (1) data classification — which machines may hold sensitive data and what sanitisation method is appropriate for each tier; (2) the disposal pathway for the hardware (which NTCRS or certified recycler, with location); (3) documentation requirements for each disposed asset; and (4) a brief explanation for management of why general waste disposal would violate both the Privacy Act 1988 and the Product Stewardship Act 2011.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · DR. FINCH IS IN THE REVIEW
blocks.text:
Before the disposal plan is finalised:
1. The office manager says 'we did a factory reset on all the machines — that's enough.' What would you tell them, and what is the specific risk if they're wrong?
2. One staff member wants to keep her old work laptop for personal use. Under the Privacy Act 1988, what data security obligations apply before the device can be transferred to personal ownership?
3. The government office considers shipping 50 non-working computers to a recycling facility overseas. What international and Australian legal considerations should they check before doing this?

question.text: A school wants to dispose of 30 old computers. Which free, government-backed Australian scheme allows them to do this responsibly?
options: The National Television and Computer Recycling Scheme (NTCRS)
The Australian Consumer Law disposal program
The Privacy Act 1988 disposal framework
The Product Safety Australia program
question.explanation: The National Television and Computer Recycling Scheme (NTCRS) provides free drop-off recycling for computers and televisions at authorised collection points across Australia, funded by product importers and manufacturers under the Product Stewardship Act 2011.

question.text: Explain why simply deleting files from a computer before donating it is insufficient, and describe one method that would constitute effective data sanitisation.
question.explanation: Award 1 mark for explaining that deleting files or emptying the recycle bin only removes the file index — the underlying data remains on the disk and can be recovered using freely available software. Award 2 marks for describing an effective method: e.g., using NIST 800-88 compliant overwriting software (like DBAN) that overwrites all storage sectors; or physical destruction of the drive via shredding or degaussing.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #1005 · REGIONAL ETHICS TECH GROUP
blocks.text: INTERN BRIEFING: A regional hospital board is reviewing its communication protocols. They currently mix email, messaging apps, phone calls, and face-to-face meetings for everything from patient referrals to emergency callouts. They've asked us to help them design a communication policy that matches the right channel to the right context — especially where privacy and safety are at stake. This is a policy design and risk analysis task.

The shift from traditional to digital communication is one of the most profound social changes technology has produced. The way we work, form relationships, and access information has been transformed in less than a generation. Understanding the trade-offs — speed vs. depth, reach vs. privacy, convenience vs. permanence — is essential for both IT professionals and digital citizens.

items.definition: Communication that occurs in real time, requiring all parties to be present simultaneously — e.g., phone calls, video conferences, face-to-face meetings.
Communication that does not require simultaneous presence — messages can be sent and responded to at different times — e.g., email, letters, recorded video.
Any communication transmitted electronically via digital networks — including email, instant messaging, social media, video conferencing, and VoIP.
The body language, facial expressions, tone of voice, and gestures that convey meaning beyond words — largely absent from text-based digital communication.

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · DR. ELARA FINCH, REGIONAL ETHICS TECH GROUP
blocks.text: Digital communication is extraordinarily powerful — and extraordinarily easy to misuse. In a hospital setting, a misread tone in an email can delay a critical patient handover. In a school, a short message stripped of context gets screenshotted and sent to 300 people. The right communication channel isn't always the fastest one. It's the one that gets the right information to the right person with the right level of privacy and context. That's policy thinking, not just technology thinking.
Comparing Communication Methods

rows: Slower — especially letters and formal documents
Higher for physical delivery; phone calls can be costly over distance
Very low cost — most digital platforms are free or subscription-based
Limited by geography and time zones for real-time communication
Physical documents can be secured; verbal conversations leave no record
Digital messages are logged, stored, and potentially accessible by third parties
Letters and documents are permanent; verbal conversations are not recorded by default
All digital communications create a permanent record — messages can be retrieved years later
Full range of body language, tone, and expression available in person
Limited in text; partial in video; absent in email and messaging
Requires physical presence or postal access
Requires internet access and device — creates digital divide for some groups

blocks.text: Impact on Work and Society

Digital communication has enabled remote work, global collaboration, and rapid information sharing at a scale previously impossible. However, it has also blurred the boundary between professional and personal life, enabled the instant spread of misinformation, and created new forms of harassment and exclusion. These are not side effects to be managed — they are design considerations that ethical IT professionals must address.

blocks.title: Scenario: The Hospital Communication Policy
blocks.scenario: Regional Health Service uses a mix of channels: urgent patient alerts go via a consumer messaging app (WhatsApp), referrals are emailed to GPs using personal Gmail accounts, administrative updates go via the hospital's internal system, and shift managers call staff directly for emergency callouts. A patient's confidential test results were accidentally shared in the wrong WhatsApp group last month.

questions.question: Identify two specific communication practices in this scenario that likely breach the Privacy Act 1988 and explain why.
Recommend a communication framework for the hospital that matches the appropriate channel to each type of communication (clinical, administrative, emergency). Justify each recommendation.
The hospital is considering banning all consumer messaging apps (WhatsApp, iMessage) for clinical communication. What are the likely benefits and unintended consequences of this policy?

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · CHANNEL SELECTION MATRIX
blocks.text: Design a 'Communication Channel Matrix' for a medium-sized IT company. The matrix must specify which communication channel (email, instant messaging, video call, phone, face-to-face) is appropriate for each scenario type, and why. Include at least 6 scenario types: e.g., urgent technical incident, performance review, client project update, team social event announcement, data breach notification, and employee grievance. For each, consider: privacy requirements, tone, urgency, and the need for a documented record.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · DR. FINCH IS IN THE REVIEW
blocks.text:
Before the hospital communication framework is finalised:
1. A clinician argues that banning WhatsApp will slow down emergency communication and put patients at risk. How do you balance communication speed against privacy obligations under the Privacy Act?
2. The Spam Act 2003 governs commercial electronic messages — does it apply to internal hospital emails? What about emails sent to patients about appointment reminders?
3. If a hospital employee sends patient data to a personal Gmail account to work from home, what specific Privacy Act obligations are potentially breached — and who is liable, the employee or the hospital?

question.text: Which of the following is the most significant privacy risk when using consumer messaging apps (like WhatsApp) for clinical communication in a hospital?
options: Messages can be delivered to the wrong recipient accidentally
The hospital cannot control data storage, access logs, or where the data is held
Messages cost money to send internationally
Video calls may have poor quality
question.explanation: Consumer apps store data on servers controlled by private companies (often overseas), outside the hospital's data governance framework. The hospital cannot audit access, control data retention, or guarantee compliance with the Privacy Act 1988 using consumer platforms.

question.text: Describe two advantages and two disadvantages of digital communication compared to face-to-face communication in a professional workplace setting.
question.explanation: Award 1 mark each for two valid advantages (e.g., instant delivery regardless of geography; asynchronous — can respond at a convenient time; creates a permanent documented record; low cost). Award 1 mark each for two valid disadvantages (e.g., lack of non-verbal cues can cause misunderstandings; creates a permanent record that can be used against you; privacy risks from data interception or misdirection; contributes to 'always on' culture blurring work-life boundaries).

lessons.title: Impacts of Technology — Unit Review Quiz

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #1006 · REGIONAL ETHICS TECH GROUP
blocks.text: INTERN REVIEW: You've completed your first rotation in the Digital Policy & Innovation team. Before you move to your next assignment, Dr. Finch needs to see that you can apply what you've learned — not just recall it. This quiz tests your ability to identify issues, reason through ethical dilemmas, and apply Australian law. Policy thinking under pressure is a core professional skill.

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · DR. ELARA FINCH, REGIONAL ETHICS TECH GROUP
blocks.text: In this industry, 'I know the theory' is never enough. Every question below reflects a situation I have dealt with in practice. Think about the law, the ethics, and the human impact — in that order. A technically correct answer that ignores the human cost is still an incomplete answer.

question.text: An employer is required under Australian law to provide employees with ergonomically safe computer workstations. Which legislation creates this obligation?
options: Privacy Act 1988
Work Health and Safety Act 2011
Australian Consumer Law
Copyright Act 1968
question.explanation: The Work Health and Safety Act 2011 (WA) places a duty on employers to provide and maintain a working environment that is safe and without risks to health — this includes ergonomic workstation requirements for computer users.

question.text: Under the Privacy Act 1988, an organisation can sell customer email addresses to a marketing company without the customer's consent, provided the data remains in Australia.
question.explanation: False. The Australian Privacy Principles prohibit using or disclosing personal information for a purpose other than the one for which it was collected, regardless of where the data is stored. Selling email addresses to a third party for marketing requires explicit consent.

question.text: Which of the following correctly describes a 'digital footprint'?
options: The physical space occupied by digital hardware in an office
The permanent trail of data created by a person's online activities
The carbon emissions produced by running data centres
The storage capacity used by a user's digital files
question.explanation: A digital footprint is the accumulation of data traces left by a person's online activities — including social media posts, search history, online purchases, and account sign-ups. It is effectively permanent and can be accessed by employers, universities, and law enforcement.

question.text: A student repeatedly sends threatening private messages to a classmate on Instagram. Under which law could this conduct be prosecuted in Australia?
options: The Privacy Act 1988
The Copyright Act 1968
The Criminal Code Act 1995 — using a carriage service to menace or harass
The Australian Consumer Law
question.explanation: Using a carriage service (which includes the internet and mobile networks) to menace, harass, or cause offence is a criminal offence under section 474.17 of the Criminal Code Act 1995, carrying a penalty of up to 3 years imprisonment.

question.text: Which Australian government program provides FREE recycling for old computers and televisions?
options: The Privacy Act Disposal Framework
The National Television and Computer Recycling Scheme (NTCRS)
The Australian Consumer Law Exchange Program
The Digital Divides Reduction Initiative
question.explanation: The National Television and Computer Recycling Scheme (NTCRS) provides free drop-off recycling for televisions and computers under the Product Stewardship Act 2011. It is funded by product importers and manufacturers.

question.text: A company is replacing 50 old desktop computers. The IT manager says 'we deleted everything and ran a factory reset — we're good to go.' Evaluate whether this is sufficient and explain what the company should have done instead.
question.explanation: Award 2 marks for correctly identifying that factory reset and deletion are insufficient — they remove the file index but not the underlying data, which can be recovered using freely available software, creating a Privacy Act breach risk. Award 2 marks for describing adequate sanitisation: NIST 800-88 compliant overwriting software (e.g., DBAN); physical destruction (shredding/degaussing) for sensitive data; and documentation of disposal for audit purposes.

question.text: Dr. Finch has been asked to advise a new online retail startup on its legal and ethical obligations regarding customer data. Using your knowledge of the Privacy Act 1988 and the Australian Privacy Principles, write a briefing note (approximately 200 words) that identifies: (a) what types of data the company will likely collect; (b) at least three specific obligations under the APPs; and (c) the consequences of a data breach, including mandatory notification requirements.
question.explanation: High-mark response should identify: relevant personal information (name, address, payment details, purchase history, browsing behaviour); at least three APPs (e.g., APP 1 — transparency via privacy policy; APP 3 — collect only what is necessary; APP 6 — use data only for original purpose; APP 11 — secure storage; APP 12 — access and correction rights); and consequences of a data breach including Notifiable Data Breaches scheme, required notification to the OAIC and affected individuals, and potential penalties. Award marks for accuracy, relevance, and quality of reasoning.

topics.title: Application Skills
topics.description: Word processing, spreadsheets, email, document design, and collaborative tools with practical tasks.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #2201 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🟡 IN PROGRESS | Sprint 4 | Assigned to: Junior Analyst

The client needs a polished project proposal document — 3 pages, professionally formatted, ready for the board by Friday. Leo's done the branding, Sloane's supplied the data tables, now it's on you to pull it together in Word. Before you touch the keyboard, you need to know every formatting tool at your disposal. A document that looks like it was typed in Notepad does not leave this office.
Word Processing — Your Document Delivery Engine

Word processing software is not just a fancy typewriter — it is the primary tool for producing professional written deliverables in every business on the planet. At Bunbury Tech Launchpad, every client proposal, technical brief, and meeting summary goes through Word or Google Docs before it goes out the door. Understanding the full feature set is not optional; it is table stakes for working in this team.

Microsoft Word and Google Docs share the same core feature set, but Word has deeper formatting control and is the industry standard for formal documents. Google Docs wins on real-time collaboration — which is why Sloane uses it for shared data summaries. Know both, and know when to reach for which one.

items.definition: Named sets of formatting (font, size, colour, spacing) applied to text in one click. Using Heading 1, Heading 2, and Normal styles consistently is what makes automatic Tables of Contents work.
A feature that combines a template document with a data source (e.g. a spreadsheet) to produce personalised copies — used for bulk letters, invoices, and certificates.
A review mode that records every edit made to a document, showing who changed what and when. Clients and managers use this to approve or reject revisions before a document is finalised.
A formatting marker that divides a document into independent sections, each of which can have its own page orientation, columns, headers, or footers.
An automatically generated list of headings and their page numbers, built from the Styles applied in the document. It updates with one click when the document changes.

blocks.text: Core Feature Categories

rows: Bold, Italic, Underline, Font size, Colour
Emphasising key terms, applying brand typography
Structuring readable body text and lists
Producing landscape tables or multi-column newsletters
Formal reports, academic submissions, legal documents
Client review cycles and internal proofreading
Data source linkage, Merge fields, Preview
Tables, Images, Charts, Smart Art, Text boxes
Embedding Sloane's data charts or Leo's brand graphics

blocks.text: Styles are the single most important feature a junior analyst ignores and a senior one depends on. If you manually bold every heading instead of applying the Heading 2 style, your Table of Contents will be empty and your document will collapse the moment the client asks for a redesign. Use styles from the first keystroke.

items.label: Microsoft Word
points: Industry standard for formal business and legal documents
Superior mail merge, macro support (VBA), and print layout control
Works offline — no internet connection required
Better for complex multi-section documents with precise formatting
Files saved as .docx — universally accepted by clients

items.label: Google Docs
points: Real-time multi-user collaboration with live cursors
Version history stored automatically in Google Drive
Accessible from any device with a browser
Commenting and suggestion mode ideal for team reviews
Integrates directly with Google Sheets for linked data

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · RORY QUINN, BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text: I have received beautifully written proposals that were rejected at the door because the formatting screamed 'student assignment'. Three things give you away every time: inconsistent heading sizes set manually instead of with Styles, page numbers missing or wrong, and a Table of Contents that lists 'Error! Bookmark not defined.' Apply Styles first. Build your structure. Then write your content. The document should hold itself together, not be held together by you hitting Ctrl+B a hundred times.
Headers, Footers, and Page Numbering

Every formal document Bunbury Tech Launchpad sends to a client includes a header with the project name and our logo, and a footer with the page number, document version, and date. This is not decoration — it is professionalism. If the client prints a 20-page proposal and drops it, they can reassemble it. If your pages are not numbered, that document is a pile of paper.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · FORMAT A PROPOSAL DOCUMENT
blocks.text: Open Word (or Google Docs). Create a new document titled 'Bunbury Tech Launchpad — Project Proposal'. Apply Heading 1 to the title, then add three sections using Heading 2: 'Project Overview', 'Scope of Work', 'Timeline'. Write two sentences of placeholder text under each heading. Insert a Table of Contents at the top. Add a footer with your name and the page number. Save as rory-proposal-draft.docx.

blocks.title: Applying Styles and Generating a Table of Contents
blocks.intro: Follow these steps to build a correctly styled document that generates an automatic Table of Contents.
steps: Open Microsoft Word and create a new blank document.
Type your document title and, with the cursor on that line, click 'Heading 1' in the Styles panel on the Home tab.
Add three section headings below it. Select each and apply 'Heading 2' from the Styles panel.
Write 2–3 sentences of body text under each heading. Leave the style on 'Normal'.
Click at the very top of the document (before the title) and press Enter to create a blank line.
Move your cursor to that blank line. Go to the References tab and click 'Table of Contents', then choose 'Automatic Table 1'.
The TOC will appear. If any heading is missing, check that you applied the correct style.
Go to Insert > Header & Footer > Header and type the document title.
Go to Insert > Header & Footer > Footer, then use Insert > Page Number to add automatic page numbering.
Save the file as rory-proposal-practice.docx.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · RORY IS REVIEWING YOUR SPRINT
blocks.text:
Before I sign off on your document deliverable, answer these three questions — and I mean think, not Google:

1. If I asked you to change every Heading 2 in a 30-page document from blue to black, what is the fastest way to do it — and why does it only take 10 seconds if you used Styles correctly?

2. A colleague sends you a revised version of your proposal with Track Changes turned on. You accept all changes and now the Table of Contents shows wrong page numbers. What do you do, and why did this happen?

3. You need to send 200 personalised invitation letters — same content, different names and addresses. Which Word feature do you use, and what two files do you need to set it up?

question.text: Which Word feature automatically generates a list of headings and page numbers that updates when the document changes?
options: Mail Merge
Track Changes
Table of Contents
Section Break
question.explanation: A Table of Contents is built automatically from the Heading styles applied in the document and can be refreshed with a single click whenever content changes.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #2202 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🔴 DUE TODAY | Sprint 4 | Assigned to: Junior Analyst

This is not a theory lesson — this is a delivery sprint. The client presentation is at 9 AM tomorrow and we need a polished two-page project brief in Word format. Leo has sent the colour palette and logo. Sloane has provided a comparison table. You are producing the finished document. I will review it at 4 PM. If the formatting is inconsistent or the TOC is broken, it comes back to you tonight.
Putting It Together — The Full Document Sprint

Practical word processing skill is not about knowing where the buttons are — it is about producing a clean, professional document under time pressure. In this sprint, you will build a complete client brief from scratch: structure, styles, table, image placeholder, header and footer, and a mail merge field. This is a realistic replica of what the Bunbury Tech Launchpad team produces every week.

Before you open Word, plan your structure. The worst mistake a junior makes is typing content and then trying to format it. Start with your Styles, build your skeleton, then fill it in. You will save thirty minutes and avoid the 'I've formatted myself into a corner' disaster at 3:45 PM.

items.definition: A version of a document containing only headings and placeholder text, used to confirm structure and navigation before writing full content.
A Word setting that prevents a single line of a paragraph from appearing alone at the top or bottom of a page, keeping paragraphs visually cohesive.
A placeholder in a mail merge template (e.g. «FirstName») that is replaced with data from a linked spreadsheet when the merge is run.
A built-in Word tool that scans the document for issues that may make it hard to read for people using screen readers — checks alt text on images, colour contrast, and heading structure.

blocks.text: Document Quality Checklist

rows: All body text in Normal, all section headings in Heading 2
Footer shows correct page number on every page
Table has header row, consistent column widths, no overflowing text
No red or blue underlines remain in the final document
Every inserted image has descriptive alt text added
File name follows naming convention = PASS

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · RORY QUINN, BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text: Every time you finish a document, run three checks before you send it. First: scroll through in Print Layout view — if anything looks wrong on screen it will look wrong on paper. Second: run the Spelling and Grammar checker, because your brain autocorrects what your eyes miss. Third: save it with a version number. 'Final.docx' is not a version number. 'Final_v2.docx' is still not a version number. Use 'clientbrief-2025-07-04-v3.docx' and you will never email the wrong version to a client again.

blocks.title: Build the Bunbury Tech Launchpad Client Brief
blocks.intro: Complete this end-to-end document production task. Allow 30–40 minutes. Work through the steps in order — do not skip ahead.
steps: Open Word. Set page margins to Narrow (Layout > Margins > Narrow) to give more working space.
Apply the 'Office' document theme (Design > Themes > Office) to set consistent fonts.
Type 'Bunbury Tech Launchpad — Client Project Brief' and apply the Heading 1 style.
Add a blank line, then type 'Prepared for: «ClientName»' — this will become your mail merge field later.
Add three sections using Heading 2: 'Project Overview', 'Deliverables', 'Project Timeline'.
Write 2–3 sentences of content under each section heading. Use Normal style for all body text.
Under 'Deliverables', insert a 3-column table with headers: 'Deliverable', 'Owner', 'Due Date'. Add 4 rows of sample data.
Go to References > Table of Contents and insert Automatic Table 1 at the top of the document (before the title).
Go to Insert > Header, type 'Bunbury Tech Launchpad | Confidential' in the header area.
Go to Insert > Footer, then Insert > Page Number > Bottom of Page > Plain Number 2.
Run Mailings > Start Mail Merge > Letters, then use Select Recipients > Type a New List. Add two test client records.
Highlight '«ClientName»' and click Insert Merge Field > ClientName. Preview the merge to confirm names appear.
Run the Accessibility Checker (Review > Check Accessibility) and resolve any issues flagged.
Save the file as lastname-clientbrief-v1.docx.

blocks.title: Mail Merge — Understanding the Data Link
blocks.defaultCode:
=== MAIL MERGE DATA SOURCE (Excel / CSV) ===

ClientName     | Organisation          | ProjectCode
---------------|----------------------|------------
Sarah Mitchell | Mitchell Accounting  | BTL-2025-04
James Okafor   | Okafor Constructions | BTL-2025-05

=== WORD TEMPLATE (simplified view) ===

Dear «ClientName»,

Thank you for engaging Bunbury Tech Launchpad on project «ProjectCode».
This brief outlines the agreed scope of work for «Organisation».

=== MERGE RESULT — COPY 1 ===

Dear Sarah Mitchell,

Thank you for engaging Bunbury Tech Launchpad on project BTL-2025-04.
This brief outlines the agreed scope of work for Mitchell Accounting.
blocks.challenge: You have a CSV with 50 client records. Write the mail merge field syntax you would use in Word to insert the client's first name, last name, and project code into the greeting line of a letter.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · MAIL MERGE SPRINT
blocks.text: Create a new Excel spreadsheet with three columns: ClientName, Organisation, and ProjectCode. Add five rows of fictional client data. Save it as merge-data.xlsx. Open a new Word document, write a short 3-sentence letter, and set up a mail merge linked to your spreadsheet. Insert merge fields for all three columns. Preview all five letters and confirm the data appears correctly. Export (finish merge) to a new document and save as merged-letters.docx.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · RORY IS REVIEWING YOUR SPRINT
blocks.text:
I have your document open. Answer these before I mark it as reviewed:

1. Your Table of Contents shows 'Error! Bookmark not defined.' for Section 3. You have not changed the headings. What are three possible causes, and how do you fix the one you would check first?

2. A client has opened your .docx on a Mac using Pages and the formatting has completely changed. What file format issue caused this, and what is a safe export format you could have used to prevent it?

3. The Accessibility Checker flagged your company logo as an issue. What specific information is missing, and why does it matter for some readers?

question.text: Explain what 'Track Changes' does in Word and describe one situation in a professional workplace where you would use it.
question.explanation: Track Changes records every insertion, deletion, and formatting change made to a document, attributing each edit to the user who made it. Typical uses include: a manager reviewing a junior's report before sending it to a client, a client providing feedback on a contract draft, or a team collaborating on a policy document where all edits must be approved before finalising.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #2203 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🟡 IN PROGRESS | Sprint 5 | Assigned to: Junior Analyst

Sloane's handed you a raw data file: 800 rows of project hours, costs, and team members across six months. The client wants a summary: total hours per team member, average cost per project, and a flag for any project that has gone over budget. You cannot do this by hand and you cannot use a calculator. You are going to learn Excel properly, right now, and then you are going to build the summary Sloane needs.
Spreadsheets — The Data Workhorse of Every Business

Microsoft Excel and Google Sheets are the most widely used business software tools in existence. Sloane uses them for data analysis and dashboards. I use them for project budget tracking, sprint velocity, and resource allocation. Even Leo uses Sheets to track client colour palette approvals. If you can only master one piece of software in your early career, make it a spreadsheet application.

A spreadsheet is not just a grid of numbers — it is a calculation engine, a database, a charting tool, and an automation platform rolled into one. The difference between someone who types values into cells and someone who builds dynamic, formula-driven workbooks is the difference between spending four hours on a report and spending twenty minutes on it.

items.definition: The address of a cell in a spreadsheet, written as a column letter followed by a row number (e.g. B4). Formulas use cell references so they recalculate automatically when data changes.
A cell reference that does not change when a formula is copied to other cells. Written with dollar signs: $B$4 always refers to cell B4, regardless of where the formula moves.
A descriptive label assigned to a cell or range of cells (e.g. 'Budget_Total') that can be used in formulas instead of cell addresses, making formulas easier to read and maintain.

An interactive summary table that automatically groups, sorts, counts, sums, or averages data from a larger dataset. Sloane uses these to turn 800-row data dumps into one-page summaries in under two minutes.
A feature that automatically changes the colour, font, or style of a cell based on its value or a formula — used to visually highlight over-budget items, deadlines, or outliers.

blocks.text: Essential Formula Categories

rows: Totalling project costs, calculating percentage splits
AVERAGE, MIN, MAX, MEDIAN, COUNT, COUNTA
Summarising sprint data, finding outliers in delivery times
Flagging over-budget projects, categorising client status
Finding a team member's hourly rate from a reference table
CONCATENATE, LEFT, RIGHT, MID, TRIM, UPPER, LOWER
Cleaning imported data, building full names from split columns
Calculating days until deadline, tracking sprint durations
Building dynamic ranges for charts and pivot tables

blocks.text: The most important formula to master first is IF. Every other logical function builds on it. Once you can write a nested IF that flags a project status, you are ready for IFS, AND, and OR combinations that will handle almost every business rule you encounter.

blocks.title: Core Spreadsheet Formulas — Excel / Google Sheets
blocks.challenge: Sloane's spreadsheet has a column (D) containing project costs and a column (E) containing the project budget. Write a formula for column F that shows 'OVER' if cost exceeds budget, 'AT RISK' if cost is within 10% of budget, and 'OK' otherwise. Hint: you will need a nested IF or IFS.

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · RORY QUINN, BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text: The single habit that separates a competent spreadsheet user from an expert is this: never hardcode a number inside a formula. If your formula says =SUM(B2:B10)*0.10, what happens when the GST rate changes? You have to find every formula and update it manually. Put 0.10 in a named cell called GST_Rate and write =SUM(B2:B10)*GST_Rate. Now when the rate changes, you change one cell and the entire workbook updates. Build your spreadsheets to be maintained, not rebuilt.

items.label: Relative Reference (e.g. B4)
points: Changes automatically when the formula is copied to another cell
If =B4*C4 is in D4, copying it to D5 gives =B5*C5
Use when you want the formula to apply row-by-row down a column
Default behaviour in both Excel and Google Sheets

items.label: Absolute Reference (e.g. $B$4)
points: Stays fixed on the same cell no matter where the formula is copied
If =B4*$C$1 is in D4, copying to D5 gives =B5*$C$1
Use for values that should not move: tax rates, fixed multipliers, lookup headers
Press F4 after clicking a cell reference to toggle the dollar signs on/off

blocks.title: Build a Project Budget Tracker
blocks.intro: Build a working budget tracker from scratch using the formulas covered in this lesson.
steps: Open Excel or Google Sheets. Name the file project-budget-tracker.xlsx.
In row 1, add headers: Project Name, Team Member, Hours, Hourly Rate, Total Cost, Budget, Status.
Enter data for five fictional projects across rows 2–6. Include a mix of over-budget and under-budget projects.
In column E (Total Cost), write a formula: =C2*D2. Copy it down for all five rows.
In column G (Status), write an IF formula that shows 'OVER BUDGET' if Total Cost exceeds Budget, and 'On Track' otherwise.
Apply Conditional Formatting to the Status column: red fill for 'OVER BUDGET', green fill for 'On Track'.
In row 8, add labels 'Total Hours' and 'Total Cost'. Use SUM formulas in the data columns.
Add a row for 'Average Hourly Rate' using the AVERAGE function.
Insert a Column chart showing Project Name on the X axis and Total Cost vs Budget as two data series.
Name the range E2:E6 as 'ProjectCosts' and use that named range in your SUM formula for the total.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · VLOOKUP TASK
blocks.text: Create a second sheet called 'Rates' with two columns: Team Member (A) and Hourly Rate (B). Enter five team members including Leo, Sparky, and Sloane with different rates. Go back to Sheet 1 and, in the Hourly Rate column, replace your manually entered rates with a VLOOKUP formula that looks up each team member's name in the Rates sheet. Change Sloane's rate on the Rates sheet and confirm the Total Cost on Sheet 1 updates automatically.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · RORY IS REVIEWING YOUR SPRINT
blocks.text:
I have your budget tracker open. Three questions before I approve it:

1. Your SUM formula in E8 returns 0 even though there are values in E2:E6. What are two possible reasons the cells might not be calculating, and how would you diagnose which one it is?

2. You used VLOOKUP with TRUE as the fourth argument. Why is this potentially dangerous for a team member lookup, and what argument should you have used?

3. A new team member joins and you need to add their rate to the Rates sheet. Your VLOOKUP range is hardcoded as A1:B5. What happens if they are added in row 6, and what is the correct way to define the lookup range to avoid this problem?

question.text: You write the formula =IF(D2>E2,"OVER BUDGET","On Track") in cell F2 and copy it down to F3:F6. What type of cell reference are D2 and E2 in this formula?
options: Absolute references — they will stay fixed on D2 and E2 in every row
Relative references — they will adjust to D3/E3, D4/E4, etc. as the formula is copied down
Named ranges — they refer to labelled areas of the spreadsheet
Mixed references — the column is fixed but the row changes
question.explanation: D2 and E2 are relative references. When copied down one row, they become D3 and E3; two rows down, D4 and E4 — and so on. This is the desired behaviour here, as each row should compare its own cost and budget values.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #2204 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🔴 SPRINT DELIVERY | Sprint 5 | Assigned to: Junior Analyst

Sloane has handed off the raw hours log — 6 team members, 3 projects, 8 weeks. The client meeting is at 2 PM. They want: total hours per project, cost per project (using each member's rate), a chart showing project comparison, and a status flag. Sloane says this should take you an hour. I am giving you 40 minutes. Make it happen.
Spreadsheet Sprint — Analysis Under Pressure

Knowing formula syntax is the theory. Building a working analysis workbook in a fixed time window is the skill. This practical task replicates a real Bunbury Tech Launchpad delivery: raw data comes in, you clean it, model it, and produce a summary that a client can read without any spreadsheet knowledge. Speed and accuracy both count — a fast answer that is wrong is worse than no answer.

The workflow for any spreadsheet analysis task is always the same: inspect the raw data first, clean it second, build formulas third, add presentation last. Analysts who skip to the formulas before checking the data produce beautiful summaries of corrupted numbers. Sloane's cardinal rule is: garbage in, garbage out.

items.definition: A spreadsheet feature that restricts what can be entered into a cell — e.g. only dates, only numbers within a range, or only items from a dropdown list. Prevents incorrect data from entering the model.
Functions that sum values in a range only where a condition (or multiple conditions) is met. SUMIF checks one condition; SUMIFS checks multiple conditions simultaneously.
A view setting that locks specified rows or columns in place so that headers remain visible as you scroll through a large dataset.
A tiny chart embedded inside a single cell, used to show a trend or pattern in a row of data without taking up a full chart area.

rows: Check for blank rows, duplicate entries, inconsistent formatting
Ctrl+End to find last row; Filter to spot blanks
Remove extra spaces, fix date formats, standardise text case
Calculate totals and averages per group
Freeze header row, hide raw data sheet, name the file correctly

blocks.title: SUMIF and SUMIFS — Conditional Totals
blocks.challenge: Your data has columns: A=Week, B=Team Member, C=Project, D=Hours, E=Hourly Rate. Write a formula to calculate Sloane's total cost across Project Beta only. Remember: cost = hours × rate, and you need the SUM of (hours × rate) for matching rows. Hint: SUMPRODUCT may help here.

blocks.title: Build the Project Hours Analysis Workbook
blocks.intro: Complete this full analysis sprint. This is a timed task — aim to finish in 40 minutes.
steps: Create a new workbook called hours-analysis.xlsx with two sheets: 'Raw Data' and 'Summary'.
On the Raw Data sheet, create columns: Week (1–8), Team Member, Project (Alpha/Beta/Gamma), Hours, Hourly Rate. Enter at least 24 rows of data (6 team members × 4 entries each).
Freeze the header row on the Raw Data sheet: View > Freeze Rows > Freeze 1 Row.
Add Data Validation to the Team Member column: restrict to a dropdown list of your 6 team members' names.
On the Summary sheet, create a table with rows for each of the three projects and columns: Project, Total Hours, Total Cost, Average Hours per Entry.
In the Total Hours column, use SUMIF to calculate hours per project from the Raw Data sheet.
In the Total Cost column, use SUMPRODUCT to calculate (hours × hourly rate) per project.
Add an IF formula in a Status column: 'REVIEW' if Total Cost exceeds 10,000, otherwise 'OK'.
Apply Conditional Formatting: red fill for 'REVIEW', green fill for 'OK'.
Insert a clustered column chart on the Summary sheet showing Total Hours and Total Cost per project.
Add sparklines in column F of the Raw Data sheet showing each team member's hours trend across weeks 1–8.
Hide the Raw Data sheet so the Summary sheet is the default view, then save the file.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · PIVOT TABLE POWER
blocks.text: On your Raw Data sheet, select the entire dataset and insert a Pivot Table on a new sheet called 'Pivot'. Set Team Member as the Row field, Project as the Column field, and Hours as the Values field (summarised by SUM). Add a second Values field for Hours summarised by AVERAGE. Format the pivot table using a built-in style. Add a Slicer for the Team Member field so you can filter the pivot table with one click.

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · RORY QUINN, BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text: A chart in a spreadsheet is a communication tool, not a decoration. Every chart you insert must have a title that tells the reader what they are looking at, axis labels that include units, and a legend if there are multiple data series. I have sat in client meetings where an analyst showed a chart with no axis labels and the client spent ten minutes asking what the numbers represented. Label everything. If the chart cannot stand alone without a verbal explanation, it is not finished.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · RORY IS REVIEWING YOUR SPRINT
blocks.text:
Your workbook is open. Walk me through these before I sign off:

1. Your SUMIF formula for Project Alpha is returning 0 but there are clearly matching rows in the raw data. You spell-check the project name — it looks right. What else could cause a text-match condition to fail silently?

2. You used a Pivot Table to summarise the data, but when you added three new rows to the raw data, the pivot table did not update. What two-step process do you follow to make sure a pivot table always reflects the latest data?

3. The client prints your Summary sheet and the chart spills off the edge of the page. What two actions can you take in Excel to fix this before printing?

question.text: Which function would you use to calculate the total hours logged only by the team member named 'Sparky' from a column of team member names?
options: =SUM(D2:D20) with a filter applied
=COUNTIF(B2:B20, "Sparky")
=SUMIF(B2:B20, "Sparky", D2:D20)
=VLOOKUP("Sparky", B2:D20, 3, FALSE)
question.explanation: SUMIF(range, criteria, sum_range) adds values in the sum_range only where the corresponding cell in range matches the criteria. =SUMIF(B2:B20, "Sparky", D2:D20) checks column B for 'Sparky' and sums the matching values in column D.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #2205 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🟡 IN PROGRESS | Sprint 3 | Assigned to: Junior Analyst

I received a forwarded email from the client this morning. It was a reply-all to an internal chain where someone on our team had said, quote, 'the client's timeline is unrealistic lol'. That someone was a junior analyst on their second week. The client relationship is now damaged, the project is at risk, and that analyst is no longer with us. Email is a professional communication tool. This lesson is not optional.
Email — The Written Record of Your Professional Life

Every email you send from a work account is a legally discoverable business record. It represents you, your team, and Bunbury Tech Launchpad. Unlike a phone call, it cannot be unsaid — it sits in servers, inboxes, and sent folders indefinitely. Writing well, organising effectively, and understanding the difference between To, CC, and BCC is not a soft skill — it is a core business competency.

Webmail services like Gmail and Outlook Online give you access to your email through a browser, with no installed software required. They also include calendaring, task lists, and collaborative document tools — making them full productivity platforms, not just message delivery systems. The Bunbury Tech Launchpad team uses Gmail for internal communication and Outlook for client-facing correspondence.

items.definition: The primary recipient(s) of the email — the people who need to read it and are expected to take action or respond.
Recipients who receive the email for information only. They are not expected to reply, but they can see who else received the message.
Recipients who receive the email but whose address is hidden from all other recipients. Used to include someone privately without revealing their involvement.
A chain of replies to an original email, grouped together in a conversation view. Threads keep the context of a conversation in one place.
A block of formatted text automatically appended to outgoing emails, typically containing name, job title, organisation, contact details, and sometimes a logo.

blocks.text: To, CC, BCC — The Rules That Protect Everyone

rows: Yes — sees all To and CC addresses
Stakeholders who need to be informed
Yes — sees all To and CC addresses
Private copy to someone not visible to others
No — only sees their own address
Sends response to the sender only
Sends response to sender AND all To/CC recipients

blocks.text: The Reply All disaster is one of the most common professional email mistakes in existence. Before you click Reply All, ask: does every person on this thread need to read my response? If the answer is no, use Reply. The client who received our internal sarcasm received it because someone hit Reply All on a message that should have been a direct reply to a colleague.

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · RORY QUINN, BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text: My personal rule for every email before I send it: read it as if the client, your manager, and a lawyer are all in the room. Would you say every word out loud in that room? If not, rewrite it. Subject lines should be specific — 'Follow-up' tells no one anything; 'BTL Project Alpha — Invoice Approval Required by Friday' tells everyone exactly what they need to do. Keep the body to three paragraphs maximum: what you need, why you need it, and what happens next. Nobody reads a twelve-paragraph email in full.

blocks.title: Webmail Platforms — Gmail vs Outlook Online

items.label: Gmail (Google Workspace)
points: Conversation threading groups all replies in one view automatically
Labels (not folders) — an email can have multiple labels
Integrates with Google Docs, Sheets, Meet, and Calendar natively
Powerful search with advanced operators (from:, subject:, has:attachment)
Filters and rules for automatic sorting and labelling

items.label: Outlook Online (Microsoft 365)
points: Folders-based organisation — traditional and familiar for corporate environments
Deep integration with Word, Excel, Teams, and SharePoint
Rules for automatic sorting, forwarding, and flagging
Focused Inbox separates important from less important emails automatically
Calendar and Tasks tightly integrated with Exchange server

blocks.title: Organise Your Inbox with Filters and Folders
blocks.intro: A disorganised inbox is a missed deadline waiting to happen. Follow these steps to set up an inbox management system.
steps: Open your Gmail or Outlook Online account.
In Gmail: click the Settings gear > See all settings > Filters and Blocked Addresses > Create new filter. In Outlook: go to Settings > View all Outlook settings > Mail > Rules.
Create a filter/rule: any email from your teacher's domain (or a fictional client domain) is automatically labelled 'Client' (Gmail) or moved to a 'Client Correspondence' folder (Outlook).
Create a second filter: any email with 'Invoice' in the subject line is starred (Gmail) or flagged (Outlook).
Create a folder or label structure: Client Correspondence, Internal, Invoices, Archive.
Drag or assign three existing emails to the appropriate folder or label.
Set up an email signature: go to Settings > Signature. Include your full name, year level, school, and a professional email sign-off.
Compose a new email to yourself with a professional subject line, a three-paragraph body, and a greeting and sign-off. CC your teacher.
Send the email and verify your signature appears correctly in the sent copy.

blocks.title: Gmail Search Operators — Finding Emails Instantly
blocks.challenge: Write a Gmail search query that finds all unread emails from any sender at a domain ending in .com.au, received after 1 January 2025, that contain an attachment. Check the Gmail search operators documentation if you need to look up the domain search syntax.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · PROFESSIONAL EMAIL SPRINT
blocks.text: Scenario: Rory has asked you to follow up with a fictional client, Sarah Mitchell from Mitchell Accounting, about an overdue invoice (Invoice #BTL-2025-04, $4,500, due 15 days ago). Write a professional email to Sarah. Use a specific subject line. Open with a polite but firm greeting, state the invoice details and overdue status, request payment or a response by end of week, and close professionally. CC Rory (rory@bunburytechlaunchpad.com). Then write a brief internal email to Rory (in a separate draft) explaining what you sent and why you worded it the way you did.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · RORY IS REVIEWING YOUR SPRINT
blocks.text:
I have read your client email before you sent it. Three questions:

1. You put both the client and Rory in the 'To' field. What is the professional convention for where Rory should appear, and what does that field choice communicate to the client about Rory's role in this correspondence?

2. Your email subject line reads 'Follow Up'. Rewrite it so it is specific, actionable, and tells the recipient immediately what the email is about.

3. You wrote 'As per my last email' in the second paragraph. Why is this phrase considered passive-aggressive in professional correspondence, and how would you rephrase it to achieve the same factual purpose without the implied accusation?

question.text: Using the BCC field on an email means all recipients can see who else received the message.
question.explanation: BCC (Blind Carbon Copy) hides the recipient's address from all other recipients. Only the sender can see who is in the BCC field. Recipients in To and CC cannot see BCC recipients at all.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #2206 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🟢 NEW | Sprint 6 | Assigned to: Junior Analyst

Leo's on leave this week. The client has requested a one-page product summary flyer and a three-slide deck overview by Thursday. Normally this is Leo's territory, but Leo has left you his Canva brand kit and a Figma component file. You are going to learn the fundamentals of document design well enough to produce something that does not embarrass us. Leo will review it when he's back, but you need to get it 90% there.
Design Principles That Every Analyst Must Know

You do not need to be Leo to produce professional-looking documents. You need to understand four design principles: contrast, repetition, alignment, and proximity — commonly abbreviated as CRAP. These principles were not invented for graphic designers; they describe how every human brain processes visual information. Violate them and your document looks amateur. Apply them and your document looks intentional.

Leo's first rule when he reviews any document: 'Did you use a grid?' White space is not wasted space — it is breathing room that directs the eye and reduces cognitive load. The instinct to fill every gap with text or colour is the number one mistake non-designers make. Less, placed deliberately, is always more powerful than more, scattered randomly.

items.definition: The visual difference between elements — such as dark text on a light background, or a large heading against small body text. Contrast creates hierarchy and draws the reader's eye to what matters most.
The positioning of elements along a consistent invisible line. Left-aligned text, centred headings, and grid-snapped images all create visual order. Random alignment creates visual noise.
Grouping related elements close together and separating unrelated elements. A label placed next to its data field is immediately understood; a label two centimetres away creates confusion.

Using the same visual style (fonts, colours, icon set, spacing) consistently throughout a document. Repetition creates unity — the reader's brain recognises the pattern and navigates more efficiently.
The visual ranking of information — the most important element should be the largest, boldest, or most prominent. Hierarchy tells the reader where to look first, second, and third.

blocks.text: Typography and Colour in Business Documents

rows: Maximum 2 typefaces per document — one for headings, one for body
Using 4+ fonts — looks unprofessional and inconsistent
Body text at 9pt — unreadable in print
1.15–1.5 for body text; generous for headings
Single spacing on dense paragraphs — fatigues the reader
Use the brand palette — maximum 3 colours in a document
Rainbow effect: using 6+ colours with no visual logic
Text must meet WCAG AA: 4.5:1 contrast against background
Light grey text on white background — fails accessibility
Minimum 1.5 cm on all sides for print documents
0.5 cm margins — text is cut off by the printer's non-print zone
Minimum 150 dpi for screen, 300 dpi for print
Stretching a small web image — appears pixelated when printed

blocks.text: Colour is a brand asset, not a personal preference. Every organisation has a brand colour palette and Leo guards Bunbury Tech Launchpad's palette fiercely. When you create a document, you open the brand kit in Canva or paste the hex codes into your application. You do not 'pick something that looks close'. Hex code #1A3A5C is not the same as #1B3B5D — and the client notices.

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · RORY QUINN, BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text: The fastest way to make any document look more professional is to remove half of what is on the page. Seriously — take your draft, remove every element that is not carrying essential information, and see what is left. Every time I try this, the document becomes clearer and more persuasive. Leo calls it 'editing to the essence'. I call it 'stop decorating and start communicating'. Your audience has thirty seconds of attention — every element on the page is competing for it.

items.label: Canva
points: Browser-based, drag-and-drop — minimal design skill required
Thousands of professional templates for flyers, presentations, and social graphics
Brand Kit feature stores colours, fonts, and logos for consistent use
Exports to PDF, PNG, MP4, or PowerPoint format
Ideal for one-page flyers, social posts, and presentation slides

items.label: Figma
points: Industry-standard UI/UX and graphic design tool
Component-based design — Leo's component library can be reused in any project
Real-time collaboration with commenting and version history
Steeper learning curve than Canva but far more design control
Used for wireframes, prototypes, and production-ready design files

blocks.title: Create a One-Page Product Summary Flyer in Canva
blocks.intro: Build a professional one-page flyer using Canva's tools and Leo's design principles.
steps: Go to canva.com and sign in or create a free account.
Click 'Create a design' and choose 'A4 Document' as the format.
Choose a clean, minimal template — avoid anything with more than 3 colours or 2 font styles.
Replace the template heading with your product or project name (e.g. 'Bunbury Tech Launchpad — App Development Services').
Replace body text with 3 short bullet points describing the service or product. Keep each bullet to one sentence.
Delete any decorative elements from the template that do not carry useful information.
Upload a simple logo or placeholder image and position it in the top-left corner.
Ensure all text is left-aligned or follows a consistent alignment — nothing should be randomly placed.
Check that the body text is at least 11pt and has sufficient contrast against the background.
Add a simple call-to-action at the bottom: 'Contact us: rory@bunburytechlaunchpad.com'.
Download the design as a PDF (Print) at 300 dpi.
Show the design to a classmate and ask: is it clear what this is and who it is for within 5 seconds?

blocks.title: Figma — Understanding the Component System
blocks.challenge: Leo's brand uses Inter font at 14pt for body text on a white (#FFFFFF) background, with text colour #AAAAAA (light grey). Look up the WCAG AA contrast ratio requirement for normal text. Does #AAAAAA on #FFFFFF meet the standard? What is the minimum contrast Leo should be using, and what text colour would you suggest instead?

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · REDESIGN A BAD DOCUMENT
blocks.text: Your teacher will provide (or you will create) a deliberately poorly designed one-page document: mismatched fonts, clashing colours, misaligned elements, overcrowded layout. Identify every design violation — name the principle it breaks (Contrast, Alignment, Proximity, Repetition) and explain what the fix is. Then redesign the document in Canva, applying all four CRAP principles. Submit both the 'before' description and the 'after' Canva export.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · RORY IS REVIEWING YOUR SPRINT
blocks.text:
Leo has reviewed your flyer remotely and sent me his notes. Before I relay them, answer these:

1. You used three different shades of blue in the document — none of them from the brand palette. Name two specific problems this creates: one visual and one business/brand-related.

2. You centred all of your body text. Leo says this is almost always wrong for body copy. Explain why left-aligned text is easier to read for multi-line body content, referencing how the human eye tracks across lines of text.

3. Your flyer has six different font sizes in use. Describe a font hierarchy system that would replace all six sizes with a maximum of three, and explain what each size level communicates to the reader.

question.text: A document uses a dark navy heading, a medium grey subheading, and light grey body text on a white background. Which design principle is most clearly violated?
options: Proximity — the elements are not grouped logically
Contrast — the text does not stand out sufficiently against the background
Repetition — inconsistent typography is used throughout
Alignment — the text is not aligned along a consistent axis
question.explanation: Light grey text on a white background has insufficient contrast, making it difficult to read — particularly for users with low vision. This violates the principle of contrast and likely fails WCAG AA accessibility standards. Dark text on a light background (or vice versa) is required for readable, accessible documents.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #2207 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🟡 IN PROGRESS | Sprint 7 | Assigned to: Junior Analyst

We have a problem. Leo updated the design brief in his local copy of the document. Sparky edited the hardware spec in a different version. You wrote the timeline in a third copy. Now we have three conflicting documents and a client meeting in two hours. This is exactly what collaborative tools are designed to prevent. Starting today, everything — and I mean everything — goes through shared tools. This lesson is the reason why.
Collaboration Tools — The Infrastructure of a Productive Team

A team that communicates by emailing document attachments back and forth is not a team — it is a collection of individuals hoping their versions eventually match. The Bunbury Tech Launchpad team uses collaborative software tools as the backbone of every project: shared documents for content, project management boards for tasks, and communication platforms for real-time discussion. The tools do not replace communication — they structure it.

Collaborative tools introduce concepts that do not exist in single-user software: version history, simultaneous editing, commenting, access permissions, and change notifications. Understanding these concepts is as important as knowing how to use the tools, because misusing them — sharing the wrong link, editing without permission, or ignoring a comment — can cause the exact document chaos the tools are designed to prevent.

items.definition: The ability for multiple users to view and edit the same document simultaneously, with changes appearing instantly on all users' screens.
An automatic record of every saved state of a document, allowing users to view, compare, and restore previous versions. Google Docs saves version history continuously; Word saves it per save action.
Controls that determine who can view, comment on, or edit a shared document. Setting the wrong permission level is a common security and collaboration error.
A visual project management tool (e.g. in Trello or Asana) that organises tasks into columns representing stages — typically To Do, In Progress, and Done — based on Agile sprint methodology.
Communication that does not require all parties to be available at the same time — such as email, comments, or task comments. Essential for teams working across different time zones or schedules.

blocks.text: The Bunbury Tech Launchpad Tool Stack

rows: Shared budget trackers, data analysis workbooks
Sprint boards, task cards, deadline tracking
Shared UI design files, component libraries
Real-time chat, file sharing, channel-based discussion
Version-controlled code, pull requests, code review

blocks.text: Trello and Asana both implement the Kanban board model — tasks are represented as cards that move through columns as they progress. The difference is complexity: Trello is simple and visual, which makes it perfect for sprint planning; Asana adds task dependencies, timeline views, and reporting, which I use for projects that span multiple teams. Sparky uses GitHub Projects for hardware firmware tasks because the development workflow matches.

items.label: Trello
points: Card-and-column Kanban board — clean and visual
Minimal setup — boards are ready to use in minutes
Power-Ups (integrations) for GitHub, Slack, Google Drive
Best for small teams and simple sprint tracking
Free tier covers most small project needs

items.label: Asana
points: Multiple views: List, Board, Timeline (Gantt), Calendar
Task dependencies — set which tasks must complete before others begin
Advanced reporting and project dashboards
Better for large projects with multiple teams and milestones
Automations can trigger actions when task status changes

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · RORY QUINN, BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text: A Trello board only works if every team member updates their cards. A shared document only works if everyone uses the same link. A version history only saves you if you name your versions when it matters. These tools do not run themselves — they require discipline. My rule for the team: if it is not on the board, it does not exist. If you completed something that is not on a card, create the card, move it to Done, and add a note. Your accountability is visible. That is a feature, not a bug.

blocks.title: Set Up a Sprint Board and Shared Document in Trello + Google Docs
blocks.intro: Build a functional sprint board and link it to a shared team document.
steps: Go to trello.com and create a free account, or sign in if you have one.
Create a new board called 'BTL Sprint 7 — App Skills Unit'.
Create four lists (columns): Backlog, To Do, In Progress, Done.
Create five task cards in the Backlog list: one for each lesson in this unit (Word Processing, Spreadsheets, Email, Document Design, Collaboration Tools).
Add a due date to each card: space them one day apart from today's date.
Assign yourself to each card using the Members feature.
Add a checklist to the 'Collaboration Tools' card with three items: Complete reading, Complete task, Answer PR review questions.
Move the 'Collaboration Tools' card to 'In Progress'.
Open Google Docs and create a new document called 'BTL Sprint 7 — Team Notes'.
Share the document: click Share, change access to 'Anyone with the link can comment', copy the link.
Add the Google Docs link as an Attachment to the Trello board description.
In Google Docs, use the Insert > Comment feature to add a comment on the first paragraph asking a fictional team member ('Leo') to review the design section.

blocks.title: Google Docs — Understanding Sharing Permissions
blocks.challenge: You have a Google Slides presentation for a client pitch. You want the client to be able to view it but not edit it. You want your manager (Rory) to be able to add comments. You want Leo to be able to edit the design slides. Describe exactly what sharing settings you would configure: what link permission level you would set, and what individual permissions you would assign to Rory and Leo.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · SIMULATE A TEAM COLLABORATION
blocks.text: With a partner (or using two browser windows / accounts), open a shared Google Doc. Person A writes a project overview paragraph. Person B uses the Comment feature to suggest a change to one sentence. Person A resolves the comment and makes the edit. Person B uses Suggest Edits mode (Edit > Suggesting) to propose a new sentence. Person A accepts the suggestion. Then: check the Version History (File > Version History > See version history) and identify the point at which each edit was made. Write a two-sentence reflection: what would have happened to these edits if you had both been editing a local Word file emailed back and forth?

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · RORY IS REVIEWING YOUR SPRINT
blocks.text:
Your Trello board is up. Before I mark this sprint complete:

1. Leo is going on leave tomorrow and needs to hand off his Figma design files to you. He shares the file with 'Anyone with the link — Editor' access and sends the link in a team Slack message. Three people outside the project are in that Slack channel. What is the security risk, and what is the correct way to share the file?

2. Your Trello card for 'Email and Webmail' has been sitting in 'In Progress' for six days. You actually completed it four days ago but forgot to update the card. Aside from looking unorganised, what practical problem does this create for the rest of the team using the board to plan their work?

3. You and Sloane are both editing the same Google Sheet at the same time. You are both in cell C5. Sloane saves a value of 4500. You type 3800 and press Enter. What happens to Sloane's value, and what feature in Google Sheets could have prevented this conflict?

question.text: Which Google Docs sharing setting allows a recipient to suggest changes to text without being able to directly edit the document?
options: Viewer
Commenter
Editor with Track Changes enabled
Restricted access
question.explanation: The 'Commenter' permission level allows recipients to add comments and, in Google Docs, to use the 'Suggesting' mode to propose edits — but they cannot directly change the document text. The document owner can then accept or reject each suggestion.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #2208 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🔴 SPRINT REVIEW | Sprint 8 | Assigned to: Junior Analyst

End of unit. No notes, no Google, no 'I think it was something like that'. This is your sprint retrospective — you either know this material or you find out what to revise. The team is only as strong as its weakest link, and I need to know if you are ready to operate independently. Six questions. Show your reasoning. Let's go.
Application Skills — Unit Review Quiz

This quiz covers all six content lessons of the Y11 Application Skills unit: word processing, spreadsheets, email, document design, and collaborative tools. Each question targets a real workplace skill. Read each question carefully — several options are designed to look similar. Take your time.

Before you start: close your notes. If you genuinely cannot answer a question, mark it and move on — come back to it at the end. Guessing randomly wastes a mark you could earn by eliminating wrong answers systematically. Use what you know.

items.definition: This quiz assesses: Word Processing Features, Word Processing Practical Task, Spreadsheet Features, Spreadsheet Practical Task, Email and Webmail, Document Design and Layout, and Collaborative Software Tools.
Multiple choice (1 mark each) and short answer (2–3 marks each). Read the mark allocation before writing — a 3-mark question requires three distinct points.

rows: Styles, TOC, Mail Merge, Track Changes

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · RORY QUINN, BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text: Every skill in this unit is one you will use in your first week at a real job. The graduate who can produce a clean Word document, build a working spreadsheet formula, write a professional email, and set up a Trello board on day one walks in with an advantage. The one who cannot does not. You have had the lessons. You have done the tasks. This quiz is just proof of the work.

question.text: You apply 'Heading 2' style to five section headings in a 20-page Word document. The manager asks you to change all five headings from blue to black. What is the fastest way to make this change?
options: Select each heading individually and change the font colour to black five times
Use Find & Replace to find 'blue text' and replace it with 'black text'
Modify the Heading 2 style definition to use black text — all five headings update automatically
Delete the headings and retype them with black font colour applied manually
question.explanation: Because Styles were used, modifying the Heading 2 style (right-click the style > Modify) changes every instance of that style throughout the document simultaneously. This is the core benefit of using Styles: global, consistent, instant updates.

question.text: Describe the purpose of Mail Merge in Word and outline the two files required to run a mail merge. Give one example of when a business would use mail merge.
question.explanation: Mail Merge combines a template document (containing fixed text and merge fields like «FirstName») with a data source (such as an Excel spreadsheet or CSV file containing the variable information for each recipient). When the merge runs, Word produces one personalised copy of the document for each row in the data source. Business examples include: sending personalised invoices to clients, producing certificates of completion for students, or generating bulk letters for a promotional campaign.

question.text: In a spreadsheet, you write the formula =SUM(B2:B10)*$C$1 in cell D2 and copy it down to D3. What does the value in $C$1 do when the formula is in D3?
options: It changes to $C$2 because the formula moved down one row
It stays fixed as $C$1 because the dollar signs make it an absolute reference
It becomes C1 (relative) because copying removes the absolute reference
The formula produces an error because absolute references cannot be copied
question.explanation: The dollar signs in $C$1 create an absolute reference. When the formula is copied to any other cell, $C$1 remains fixed — it will always refer to cell C1. Only the relative part of the formula (B2:B10, which becomes B3:B11 in D3) adjusts with the copy.

question.text: Explain the difference between the CC and BCC fields in an email. Describe a specific workplace situation where using BCC would be appropriate, and explain why CC would not be suitable in that situation.
question.explanation: CC (Carbon Copy) sends a visible copy to additional recipients — all To and CC recipients can see each other's email addresses. BCC (Blind Carbon Copy) sends a hidden copy — BCC recipients are invisible to all other recipients. A workplace example where BCC is appropriate: you are sending a follow-up email to an overdue client and want to keep your manager informed without revealing to the client that they are being monitored. Using CC would be unsuitable here because the client would see the manager's address, which could affect the relationship or escalate the situation unnecessarily.

question.text: Which design principle is described by this rule: 'group related elements close together and leave space between unrelated elements'?
options: Contrast
Repetition
Alignment
Proximity
question.explanation: Proximity is the principle that related items should be grouped visually close together, while unrelated items should have clear space between them. This helps readers understand which pieces of information belong together without needing to read all the text first.

question.text: A team of four people is working on the same report. Explain TWO specific advantages of using Google Docs instead of emailing a Word document as an attachment. In your answer, refer to features of Google Docs that make collaboration more effective.
question.explanation: 1. Real-time collaboration: all four team members can edit the document simultaneously, with each person's cursor visible and changes appearing instantly — eliminating the problem of conflicting versions created by separate local copies. 2. Version history: Google Docs automatically saves a complete version history, allowing the team to see who changed what and when, and to restore any previous version if an edit is accidentally deleted or incorrect — this is not possible with emailed attachments unless someone manually tracks versions.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · RAPID-FIRE SKILLS CHECK
blocks.text:
Complete all five tasks as quickly and accurately as possible. Timer starts now:
1. Open a new Word document. Apply Heading 1 to a title and Heading 2 to two sections. Insert a Table of Contents.
2. Open Excel or Sheets. In A1:A5, enter five numbers. In B1, write a formula using IF to show 'High' if A1 > 50, 'Low' otherwise. Copy it down to B5.
3. Write a professional one-paragraph email (in a text doc) to a fictional client asking them to approve an invoice. Use a specific subject line, greeting, and sign-off.
4. In Canva, create a blank A4 document and place a heading, a short paragraph, and a simple shape, all left-aligned.
5. In Trello, create a board with three lists and add one card to each list.
Submit screenshots of all five completed items.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · RORY IS REVIEWING YOUR SPRINT
blocks.text:
Quiz done. Sprint closed. Three final questions before the retrospective:

1. Which lesson in this unit did you find most difficult, and specifically what concept or skill gave you the most trouble? How would you approach learning it differently if you were starting this unit again?

2. Name one feature from ANY tool covered in this unit that you had not used before this unit and that you think you will genuinely use in a real job or project. Explain why it is useful.

3. If a new junior analyst joined Bunbury Tech Launchpad tomorrow and you had to teach them the single most important habit from this entire unit — the one thing that would prevent the most mistakes — what would it be, and why?

topics.title: Project Management
topics.description: Planning, documenting, and evaluating a digital solution project.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #3001 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🟡 IN PROGRESS | Sprint 1 | Assigned to: Junior Analyst

We've just landed a new client — a local community sports club that needs a digital booking system for their courts and facilities. Leo's ready to start designing, Sparky is scoping the hardware requirements, and Sloane will handle the data layer. But none of that starts until YOU deliver a solid project plan. I need a project brief, a timeline, and a clear scope document on my desk before anyone writes a single line of code. Let's move.
Why Planning Comes Before Everything Else

At Bunbury Tech Launchpad, the number one cause of project failure isn't bad code — it's bad planning. When we skip the planning phase, we end up building the wrong thing, running out of time, or blowing the client's budget on features they never asked for. A solid plan is your licence to start building.

Planning a digital solution means defining what you are building, why you are building it, who it is for, and how long it will take. These four questions must be answered before Leo touches Figma or Sparky orders a single component. If you can't answer them, the project doesn't start — it's that simple.

We work in a structured environment here. Every project at the Launchpad goes through a planning phase that produces three key artefacts: a project brief, a scope statement, and a project timeline. These documents protect you, protect the client, and protect the team.
